content.problemStatement:
A very caring mother has NNN cookie jars with her. Each jar contains a different type of cookie.

There are AiA_iAi​ cookies in the iii-th jar.

The mother wanted to give some cookies to her KKK children, and she decided to only distribute cookies **of a single type**. That is, she'll choose **exactly one** of the NNN jars and distribute the cookies within it.

She'd like to ensure a couple of things:


Each child should receive **at least** 111 cookie.
Each child should also receive an **equal number** of cookies, in the spirit of fairness.
Under the above two conditions, what is the **minimum** number of "wasted" cookies, i.e, cookies that remain in the chosen jar after distribution?

If it is not possible to give out any cookies at all, print −1-1−1 instead.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the number of cookie jars and the number of children.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — where AiA_iAi​ is the number of cookies in the iii-th jar.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line a single integer denoting the minimum number of cookies that will go to waste.

If it is not possible to distribute the cookies at all, output −1-1−1 instead.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and K – the number of cookie jars and the number of children. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, where Ai is the number of cookies in the i‑th jar.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible number of wasted cookies. If it is impossible to give every child at least one cookie, output -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Choose jar with 7 cookies. Each child gets floor(7/3)=2 cookies, waste = 7%3 = 1. Test case 2: No jar has at least 4 cookies, so impossible. Test case 3: K=1, waste is always 0. Test case 4: Minimum waste is 2024%1000 = 24.
First test: jar with 10 cookies gives waste 10%5=0. Second test: both jars have fewer than 8 cookies, so answer is -1.
Jars with 12 and 18 cookies give waste 0 (12%6=0, 18%6=0). Minimum waste is 0.

solution.approach: For a fixed jar with a cookies we can give each child the same integer number x of cookies. The conditions are: x ≥ 1 and K·x ≤ a. The largest feasible x is floor(a / K). Using this x gives the smallest waste because waste = a – K·x = a % K. Therefore for each jar with a ≥ K the waste is a % K, otherwise the jar cannot be used. The answer is the minimum a % K over all jars with a ≥ K. If no such jar exists the answer is -1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T·N) overall.
